content.text: JavaScript is a dynamically typed language — a variable can hold any type of value, and types can change at runtime. Understanding what types exist and how JavaScript silently converts between them prevents an entire class of subtle bugs that are especially easy to introduce in React applications.
JavaScript has seven primitive types plus one compound type:
Integers and floats share one type
Arrays and functions are objects too
Primitives (string, number, boolean, null, undefined, bigint, symbol) are copied by value. Objects are copied by reference.
operator returns the type of a value as a string:

typeof 'hello'       // 'string'
typeof 42            // 'number'
typeof true          // 'boolean'
typeof undefined     // 'undefined'
typeof null          // 'object'  ← known bug in JS (null is NOT an object)
typeof {}            // 'object'
typeof []            // 'object'  ← arrays are objects
typeof function(){}  // 'function'
typeof Symbol()      // 'symbol'
typeof 1n            // 'bigint'
is a longstanding bug in JavaScript that cannot be fixed without breaking existing code. To check for null specifically, use
To check if a value is an array, use
In any boolean context (if statements, ternaries, logical operators), JavaScript automatically converts values to
. There are only six falsy values — everything else is truthy:

// The 6 falsy values:
false
0
-0
0n        // BigInt zero
''        // empty string
null
undefined
NaN

// Everything else is truthy, including:
'0'       // the string '0' — truthy!
[]        // empty array — truthy!
{}        // empty object — truthy!
-1        // any non-zero number — truthy!
This matters constantly in React. Rendering
on screen instead of nothing, because
is falsy but JSX renders it as text.
Type coercion is JavaScript automatically converting a value from one type to another in order to complete an operation. It happens implicitly in comparisons and arithmetic.

// When one operand is a string, + concatenates:
'5' + 3      // '53'  (3 is coerced to string)
'5' + true   // '5true'
'5' + null   // '5null'

// Arithmetic operators (-, *, /) coerce strings to numbers:
'5' - 3      // 2
'5' * 2      // 10
'10' / 2     // 5
'abc' - 1    // NaN (cannot parse 'abc' as a number)
operator coerces types before comparing. The
operator never coerces — both the value and the type must match:

// Loose equality == (type coercion happens)
0 == false       // true  (false coerces to 0)
1 == true        // true  (true coerces to 1)
'' == false      // true  (both coerce to 0)
null == undefined // true  (special rule)
'5' == 5         // true  ('5' coerces to 5)

// Strict equality === (no coercion)
0 === false      // false (different types)
'5' === 5        // false (different types)
null === undefined // false (different types)
5 === 5          // true
comparisons produce results that are very hard to predict and reason about. React codebases and modern JavaScript style guides universally ban

// String to Number
Number('42')        // 42
Number('3.14')      // 3.14
Number('')          // 0  ← surprising!
Number('abc')       // NaN
parseInt('42px')    // 42  (stops at non-digit)
parseFloat('3.14x') // 3.14
+('42')             // 42  (unary + operator)

// Number to String
String(42)          // '42'
(42).toString()     // '42'
`${42}`             // '42'

// To Boolean
Boolean(0)          // false
Boolean('')         // false
Boolean('hello')    // true
Boolean([])         // true  (empty array is truthy!)
!!value             // same as Boolean(value) — common shorthand
Both represent the absence of a value but with different meanings:
— JavaScript's default "not yet set." A declared variable that hasn't been assigned, a function parameter that wasn't passed, a missing object property — these all return
— an intentional "nothing here." You set something to
explicitly to say "this has no value right now." Common in React for state that hasn't loaded yet (
is a special number value that represents an invalid numeric result. It is produced when arithmetic fails:

Number('abc')  // NaN
0 / 0          // NaN
Math.sqrt(-1)  // NaN

// The bizarre property of NaN: it's not equal to itself
NaN === NaN    // false  ← NaN is the only value not equal to itself

// Check for NaN with Number.isNaN (not the global isNaN)
Number.isNaN(NaN)       // true
Number.isNaN('abc')     // false  (it's a string, not NaN)
isNaN('abc')            // true   ← old global isNaN coerces first — avoid it
Primitives vs Objects: Value vs Reference
Primitive values are copied when assigned or passed to a function. Objects are not copied — both variables point to the same object in memory:
This is critical for React. When you update state, you must create a
object (not mutate the existing one), because React uses reference equality to detect changes. If you mutate the existing object, the reference stays the same and React won't see the update:
Type Coercion in React: Common Pitfalls
Comparing strings from inputs to numbers
JavaScript has 7 primitive types and 1 object type. Arrays and functions are objects.
Primitives are copied by value; objects are copied by reference. React relies on reference equality for state change detection — always create new objects when updating state.
